repository_summary: oss-maintainer-copilot is a Python repository focused on issue triage, PR review preparation, release drafting. A maintainer operations toolkit for GitHub repositories with structured issue triage, PR briefs, release drafts, repository intelligence, and contributor onboarding guidance.
first_session_goal: Get the project running locally, then trace one maintainer workflow from `.github/workflows/` through the CLI and checked-in outputs.

setup_checkpoints.success_signal: You can activate a local environment without errors.

setup_checkpoints.purpose: Run the documented project setup command.
setup_checkpoints.success_signal: The command completes without blocking errors.

setup_checkpoints.purpose: Install the project and development dependencies.
setup_checkpoints.success_signal: The package and development tools install successfully.

setup_checkpoints.purpose: Install the project and development dependencies.
setup_checkpoints.success_signal: The package and development tools install successfully.

setup_checkpoints.purpose: Verify the current workflow outputs stay stable.
Check style and lint expectations before opening a pull request.
setup_checkpoints.success_signal: Ruff finishes without reporting blocking issues.

reading_order.title: Orient On The Repository
reading_order.objective: Read the top-level docs to understand the maintainer workflows and public outputs.
reading_order.completion_signal: You can explain the repository purpose and name the core maintainer workflows.

reading_order.title: Confirm Local Setup
reading_order.objective: Use the documented setup files and commands to prepare a working development environment.
reading_order.completion_signal: You know which file controls dependencies and which commands are part of the local setup path.

reading_order.title: Inspect The Automation Entry Points
reading_order.objective: Start here to understand how repository events are wired into maintainer workflows.
reading_order.completion_signal: You know when to visit .github/workflows/ and what it owns in the repository.

reading_order.title: Read The User-Facing Examples
reading_order.objective: Start here if you want the fastest overview of what the project produces today.
reading_order.completion_signal: You know when to visit examples/ and what it owns in the repository.

reading_order.title: Learn The Stable Contracts
reading_order.objective: Start here to learn expected behavior before changing schemas or workflow heuristics.
reading_order.completion_signal: You know when to visit tests/ and what it owns in the repository.

reading_order.title: Trace The Core Implementation
reading_order.objective: Start here if you want to extend issue triage, PR briefs, release drafting, or repo intelligence.
reading_order.completion_signal: You know when to visit src/ and what it owns in the repository.

reading_order.title: Ship A Narrow First Change
reading_order.objective: Choose one narrow maintainer workflow improvement, then trace it from automation to output examples and implementation.
reading_order.completion_signal: Your first change touches the workflow logic and the public-facing artifacts that document it.

contributor_tracks.name: Workflow Output Stewardship
contributor_tracks.fit_for: Contributors who want to improve examples, fixtures, or output stability before touching deeper logic.
first_tasks: Refresh checked-in example outputs when workflows change.
Add regression fixtures for edge cases maintainers want to keep stable.

contributor_tracks.name: Heuristics And Schemas
contributor_tracks.fit_for: Contributors comfortable making small, typed changes to classification logic and output models.
first_tasks: Refine a narrow heuristic and add a focused regression test.
Extend a schema additively before changing public output contracts.

contributor_tracks.name: Automation And Maintainer Operations
contributor_tracks.fit_for: Contributors who want to improve GitHub workflows, permission scopes, or maintainer-facing automation behavior.
first_tasks: Review workflow triggers and permission scopes for least privilege.
Tighten comment or artifact handling without widening automation behavior unexpectedly.

starter_tasks.title: Refresh An Example Flow
starter_tasks.reason: Example outputs are the fastest way for maintainers and evaluators to understand the product.

starter_tasks.title: Add A Regression Fixture
starter_tasks.reason: Maintainers need stable outputs before they trust automation in triage, review, or release workflows.

starter_tasks.title: Improve One Narrow Heuristic
starter_tasks.reason: Small deterministic improvements compound well when they ship with fixtures and examples.

starter_tasks.title: Tighten One Workflow Surface
starter_tasks.reason: Maintainer automation becomes more trustworthy when permission scope and artifact handling stay explicit.

escalation_notes: Ask a maintainer before renaming public output fields, changing checked-in example expectations, or widening automation behavior.
Escalate changes that affect release note wording, issue labels, or contributor-facing output stability.
Treat GitHub workflow permission changes as maintainer-review items, even when the code change seems small.
Favor additive schema evolution and narrow heuristics before proposing broader refactors.
Escalate onboarding-doc rewrites when they change the recommended contributor path or maintainer expectations.
